format_detection.evidence: Page 1 is written in continuous prose with extended paragraphs and dialogue embedded using quotation marks (e.g., Alice thinking “and what is the use of a book…”). There are no screenplay sluglines (INT./EXT.), centered ALL-CAPS character cues, or transitions; instead the text uses narrator exposition and interior thought like “thought Alice to herself.”
genre_signals: Talking anthropomorphic White Rabbit with a waistcoat-pocket and watch
Surreal portal/descent fantasy (rabbit-hole, falling down a deep well with cupboards and maps)

licensing_friction.evidence: The uploaded source is explicitly identified as “Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland, NOVEL,” a work that is in the public domain. Nothing in the provided excerpts indicates modern rights holders, permissions language, or adaptation restrictions. Based on the text as presented here, there is no sign of contested or unclear rights.

latent_choice_architecture.evidence: The opening establishes immediate, natural decision thresholds: Alice considers whether “the pleasure of making a daisy-chain would be worth the trouble,” then chooses to pursue the Rabbit (“burning with curiosity, she ran across the field after it”) and then commits again (“down went Alice after it, never once considering how in the world she was to get out again”). The hall sequence is intrinsically option-rich: “There were doors all round the hall, but they were all locked,” she finds “a tiny golden key,” a “little door,” and later a bottle labeled “DRINK ME,” all of which are clear interactive affordances. Midpoint text explicitly offers directional branching via the Cat: “In that direction… lives a Hatter… and in that direction… lives a March Hare. Visit either you like.”

bounded_agency.evidence: Alice’s core is consistent across excerpts: curious, assertive, and rule-testing while still childlike and polite when she tries (“I don’t quite understand you,” she said, “as politely as she could”). She repeatedly challenges nonsense and authority rather than transforming into a different person: “It proves nothing of the sort!” and later “I won’t!” when ordered to be silent. User agency can naturally express *how* she pursues curiosity (cautious vs impulsive, polite vs confrontational) without changing that she is fundamentally inquisitive and self-possessed.

emotional_range.evidence: Multiple registers are present in the excerpts: boredom/restlessness (“very tired… having nothing to do”), wonder/curiosity (“burning with curiosity,” “the loveliest garden you ever saw”), comedy/absurdity (the Cat’s logic about madness; “a grin without a cat!”), and fear/anger/escalation in the court (“Off with her head!”; “half of fright and half of anger”). There is also tenderness/nostalgia in the ending frame (“It was a curious dream… now run in to your tea”) and the sister’s reflective, wistful coda about Alice’s future.

recognizability.evidence: The excerpts include highly distinctive, iconic elements that are immediately identifiable on the page: “a White Rabbit… ‘Oh dear! Oh dear! I shall be late!’,” the bottle labeled “DRINK ME,” “the March Hare and the Hatter… having tea,” and the courtroom with “Off with her head!” Even without relying on outside knowledge, the text itself presents a dense cluster of signature characters/props that function as instant recognition anchors for users encountering the IP.

replayability_hook.evidence: The world repeatedly frames progress as navigation through arbitrary rules and spatial/logic gates, which maps cleanly to replayable paths: “There were doors all round the hall,” a key that fits only one door, and a labeled consumable (“DRINK ME”) that implies alternate outcomes depending on compliance. The Cat explicitly legitimizes divergent routes (“Visit either you like”), and the setting normalizes contradictory logic (“we’re all mad here”), which supports multiple valid playthroughs without breaking the premise. Even the ending reinforces a ‘dream-adventure’ container that can accommodate variation (“such a curious dream… strange Adventures”).

ip_audit.editorial_mitigation: No mitigation required for Phase 1 because no criterion is weak in the provided sections. The main practical safeguard before Phase 2 is to preserve Alice’s consistent core (curious, candid, increasingly assertive) while converting the hall/door/key/bottle sequence and the Cat’s directional prompt into three planned, consequence-forward branching choices, ensuring each branch still lands back into the story’s ‘mad logic’ without needing new canon.

branch_dimensions.description: Whether Alice acts immediately on curiosity/instinct or pauses to observe, plan, and control outcomes.
Whether Alice treats Wonderland’s labels/rules as binding guidance or as puzzles to probe, subvert, and reinterpret.

entry_point_diagnosis.editorial_diagnosis:
The opening’s first two paragraphs are low-stakes mood-setting: Alice is bored, it’s hot, she considers a daisy-chain. A new user can’t feel urgent consequence inside 60 seconds because nothing is threatened and no immediate problem is posed—only restlessness.

Stakes begin to become legible only when the White Rabbit does something impossible (talks, carries a watch) and Alice’s body commits to action—she springs up and runs. That moment contains (a) a clear goal (catch the Rabbit), (b) a clear ticking pressure ("I shall be late!"), and (c) an irreversible commitment that can plausibly go wrong (chasing into unknown space).

Everything before that is cut because it functions as prologue/backdrop rather than playable tension. Wonderland’s tone can be reintroduced through sensory detail and Alice’s impulsive competence in-motion, instead of a static riverbank setup.

format_specific_cut.cut_point: Chapter 1 — paragraph beginning: “...but when the Rabbit actually took a watch out of its waistcoat-pocket...” (within Event 1: Alice notices the White Rabbit)
format_specific_cut.original_before_cut: 2 paragraphs before the cut (riverbank boredom + daisy-chain consideration)
format_specific_cut.cut_eliminates: Passive setup, weather/setting description, internal musing without consequence, pre-inciting boredom frame
format_specific_cut.must_reintroduce: That Alice starts in an ordinary, sleepy world; her sister nearby; the heat/drowsiness baseline that makes the Rabbit’s impossibility pop. Reintroduce via a quick sensory contrast (hot bank → cool hedge shadow) and Alice’s immediate, physical reaction rather than explanation.

entry_point_diagnosis.cold_open:
Heat shimmers off the river stones, and your stockings stick to the back of your knees as you lean over the grass, half-listening to your sister’s page-turning.

Then a White Rabbit flashes past so close you catch the clean, sharp scent of crushed clover—and it mutters, plainly, like a person: “Oh dear! Oh dear! I shall be late!”

You don’t freeze. You lunge up, skirt snagging on a thistle, because the Rabbit has a waistcoat-pocket. Because it pulls out a watch and checks it with frantic dignity, as if being late could matter to a rabbit.

Is this a trick? A dream? Or the first real thing that’s happened all day?

The Rabbit darts toward the hedge, and the shadow beneath it looks like a mouth.

You break into a run before you can talk yourself out of it.
entry_point_diagnosis.emotional_promise: The emotional promise of this cold open is: pursuit. A user arrives feeling restless and wanting to chase the impossible before it vanishes.

setup.source_moment: Cut-in at: “...but when the Rabbit actually took a watch out of its waistcoat-pocket...” → Alice starts to her feet and runs; immediately sees it “pop down a large rabbit-hole under the hedge.”
setup.why_it_qualifies: This is the first genuine tension: an impossible stimulus (talking rabbit with a watch) creates a clear, urgent goal (catch the Rabbit) under time pressure (“I shall be late!”). It also contains an irreversible commitment (chasing into the hedge/rabbit-hole) that can plausibly go wrong.

escalation.source_moment: Alice goes down the rabbit-hole “never once considering how in the world she was to get out again,” falls, lands, and continues the chase into the lamp-lit hall; discovers all doors locked; finds the glass table, the tiny golden key, and the 15-inch door; glimpses the garden but can’t fit.
escalation.why_it_qualifies: Stakes compound from pursuit to entrapment. Visible goal becomes concrete: get through the small door into the garden (and implicitly regain agency/exit the hall). Clear obstacle: her body size and the missing/insufficient access (locked doors; too-small doorway; key/door mismatch). The hall puzzle formalizes the session’s playable problem.

breath.source_moment: After eating the cake, Alice grows and does the comic bit: “Good-bye, feet!” planning to send her own feet Christmas boots by carrier; “Oh dear, what nonsense I’m talking!”
breath.why_it_qualifies: This is a clean wonder/absurdity release after sustained puzzle-frustration: a playful, imaginative monologue that rewards the player with tonal levity and a vivid body-comedy image (distance from her own feet). It lands naturally as a ‘reward’ beat before the next pressure spike.

twist.source_moment: Rabbit returns “Oh! the Duchess!”; Alice, desperate, tries to ask for help (“If you please, sir—”) and the Rabbit bolts, dropping gloves and fan; Alice takes them and begins the identity spiral while fanning: “If I’m not the same, the next question is, Who in the world am I?”
twist.why_it_qualifies: Moral-weight moment is framed as a self-positioning choice under authority: does Alice attempt polite compliance/appeal to power, seize what power drops (gloves/fan) and ‘play a role,’ or reject the whole status game? There’s no correct answer because each path trades safety/legibility against self-respect and autonomy, and each can plausibly worsen her situation (being labeled a servant, losing the Rabbit, or escalating threat).

resolution.source_moment: Immediate arc resolves as: Alice has fully entered the hall puzzle loop (size changes, key access, garden desire) and now carries the Rabbit’s fan/gloves—an explicit ‘authority costume’ prop—while her internal question crystallizes: “Who in the world am I?”
resolution.why_it_qualifies: We can close Session 1 by resolving the initial pursuit arc into a new, clearer problem-state: she’s no longer simply chasing; she’s trapped in a system (rules/labels/size) and must decide how to present herself within it. The seed for Session 2 is planted: the dropped gloves/fan + identity instability set up being mistaken for “Mary Ann” and coerced by Rabbit-shaped authority later.

branching_choice_1.source_moment: Cut-in at: “...but when the Rabbit actually took a watch out of its waistcoat-pocket...” → Alice starts to her feet and runs; sees it pop down a large rabbit-hole under the hedge.
branching_choice_1.what_this_choice_tracks: impulse_vs_deliberation (how you commit to the chase when the impossible appears).
branching_choice_1.narrative_setup: A white rabbit in a waistcoat flashes a watch like it belongs to him, then gasps about being late. Your body moves before your mind catches up, and the hedge ahead is already swallowing him. The moment feels like a door that will close forever if you blink.
branching_choice_1.choice_question: How do you commit to the chase?

option_a.text: You sprint after him and dive for the rabbit-hole the instant you see it.
option_a.downstream_effect: *You enter the fall with momentum and lose any chance to mark your way back.*

option_b.text: You keep him in sight but slow just long enough to clock landmarks and the shape of the hedge.
option_b.downstream_effect: *You enter the hole a heartbeat later with a mental map and a steadier grip on your fear.*

option_c.text: You call out to him first and watch how he reacts before you commit to the hole.
option_c.downstream_effect: *You learn whether the Rabbit acknowledges you at all, but you risk losing him in the hedge.*

branching_choice_1.all_paths_arrive_at: You go down after the Rabbit and the world becomes a long, impossible fall.

expressive_choices.source_moment: Alice falls down the well with time to notice shelves, maps, pictures, and the empty marmalade jar.
expressive_choices.narrative_lead_in: Down, down, down—slow enough to think, too fast to stop. Cupboards slide past like a hallway turned vertical, and your thoughts skitter for something to hold.
expressive_choices.choice_question: What do you focus on as you fall?

option_a.text: You inventory every odd object you pass, as if curiosity can be a handhold.
option_a.downstream_effect: Your narration turns bright and precise, and you pocket a few concrete details to cling to later.

option_b.text: You try to calculate how you’ll get out, because panic loves an unanswered question.
option_b.downstream_effect: Your narration tightens into problem-solving, and the fall feels like a trap you’re already planning to escape.

option_c.text: You talk yourself up like it’s a story you’ll tell at home, because pride is steadier than fear.
option_c.downstream_effect: Your narration becomes performative and brave, and the danger reads as an adventure you refuse to be humbled by.

expressive_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: The fall ends in a thump, and you spring up into a dark passage with the Rabbit still ahead.

expressive_choices.source_moment: Alice shrinks, realizes the key is on the table, fails to climb, and cries—then scolds herself and notices the glass box with “EAT ME.”
expressive_choices.narrative_lead_in: For one shining second you’re the right size—then the key glints unreachable on the glass table, smug as a star. Your throat tightens, tears come hot and humiliating, and the hall echoes with your own smallness.
expressive_choices.choice_question: How do you treat your panic?

option_a.text: You speak to yourself gently and let the tears pass like weather.
option_a.downstream_effect: The tone softens into self-compassion, making the hall feel less like it’s judging you.

option_b.text: You scold yourself sharply, as if harshness can snap you back into control.
option_b.downstream_effect: The tone turns brisk and biting, and your inner voice becomes another obstacle to push through.

option_c.text: You go quiet and practical, treating your feelings as irrelevant data.
option_c.downstream_effect: The tone flattens into numb focus, and the puzzle becomes the only thing you allow to matter.

expressive_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: Under the table, you spot the glass box and the cake marked “EAT ME.”

expressive_choices.source_moment: After eating the cake, Alice grows and does the comic bit: “Good-bye, feet!” planning to send her own feet Christmas boots.
expressive_choices.narrative_lead_in: Your body stretches away from itself until your feet feel like distant acquaintances. The absurdity arrives like a gift: you can either laugh with it or fight it.
expressive_choices.choice_question: What do you do with the absurdity of your own growing body?

option_a.text: You lean into it and play along, making up the most ridiculous practical plan you can.
option_a.downstream_effect: The tone turns playful and imaginative, and wonder briefly outruns fear.

option_b.text: You try to regain dignity by speaking plainly and refusing to indulge the nonsense.
option_b.downstream_effect: The tone becomes controlled and self-respecting, as if composure is a kind of armor.

option_c.text: You get irritated at yourself for spiraling and treat the humor like a betrayal of seriousness.
option_c.downstream_effect: The tone sharpens into frustration, and the comedy reads as the world mocking you.

expressive_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: Your head hits the roof, the key is in your hand again, and the garden door is still hopelessly small.

branching_choice_2.source_moment: Rabbit returns panicking about the Duchess; Alice begins “If you please, sir—” and the Rabbit flees, dropping gloves and fan; Alice takes them and begins the identity spiral while fanning.
branching_choice_2.what_this_choice_tracks: self_definition_vs_external_labels (how you respond when authority’s role and costume are offered to you).
branching_choice_2.narrative_setup: Pattering feet cut through your tears, and the White Rabbit appears dressed like he belongs to rules you don’t understand. He’s terrified of someone called the Duchess, which means there’s a hierarchy here—and you are not at the top of it. When you try to speak, he startles, bolts, and leaves behind his gloves and fan like dropped permission.
branching_choice_2.choice_question: With authority fleeing and its costume on the floor, what do you do?

option_a.text: You chase after him calling “sir” and ask for help as politely as you can manage.
option_a.downstream_effect: *You position yourself as a supplicant within his world’s etiquette, inviting him to define you as “someone beneath him.”*

option_b.text: You shout after him and demand he stop treating you like a problem to run from.
option_b.downstream_effect: *You assert your own name and voice against his status, risking that this world answers boldness with punishment.*

option_c.text: You stay silent and put on the gloves while you fan yourself, taking whatever advantage his role can buy you.
option_c.downstream_effect: *You accept the power of the costume over the truth of you, making it easier for others to mistake you for what they expect.*

branching_choice_2.moral_weight_confirmation: Each option trades dignity, safety, and agency differently: politeness may secure aid but costs self-definition, confrontation preserves self-respect but escalates danger, and opportunism increases survival leverage while deepening the identity fracture.
branching_choice_2.talkability_test: Players can argue for days whether it’s braver to ask nicely, to call out cowardice, or to use the dropped symbols of rank—because each choice can be read as either strength or surrender.

branching_choice_3.source_moment: Alice fans herself with the Rabbit’s fan and spirals: “If I’m not the same… Who in the world am I?” while testing her knowledge (Ada/Mabel; wrong arithmetic/geography).
branching_choice_3.what_this_choice_tracks: rule_following_vs_rule_testing (how you decide to verify your identity when your memory and the world’s labels won’t agree).
branching_choice_3.narrative_setup: The fan cools your face, but it also cools your certainty—each breath feels like it belongs to someone else. You try to prove you’re you with schoolroom facts, and the facts slide sideways in your mouth. If the rules you learned can’t confirm you, you have to choose what counts as proof.
branching_choice_3.choice_question: How do you decide to test who you are—right now?

option_a.text: You cling to the old rules and recite what you were taught until something finally comes out correct.
option_a.next_session_opens: Session 2 opens with you trying to behave “properly” under the next command, even as Wonderland keeps contradicting your lessons.

option_b.text: You treat the wrong answers as clues and start experimenting with new patterns instead of old lessons.
option_b.next_session_opens: Session 2 opens with you actively probing commands and labels, ready to test the next instruction like a puzzle rather than a law.

option_c.text: You stop reciting and simply watch yourself—breath, hands, voice—waiting to see what stays consistent.
option_c.next_session_opens: Session 2 opens with you holding back and observing the returning footsteps, letting others reveal what role they think you are before you respond.

consequence_map_choice_1.tracked_dimension: impulse_vs_deliberation (how you commit to the chase when the impossible appears).

path_a.immediate_effect: Alice hits the rabbit-hole at a run; the hedge tears her pinafore and she tumbles in without grabbing anything—no branch, no root, no edge—so the entrance vanishes above like a snapped lid.
path_a.current_session_echo: During the fall, she has no 'last safe image' to cling to; she snatches at passing cupboards and maps but misses them, so when she later reaches the Hall of Doors she defaults to frantic trial-and-error (rattling door-handles, grabbing at the first bottle she sees) rather than methodically surveying.
path_a.next_session_payoff: Session 2, Event 24 (Rabbit mistakes her for Mary Ann): the Rabbit’s first line lands as a barked order—“Mary Ann! run home this moment, and fetch me a pair of gloves!”—and Alice’s body starts to move before she thinks, mirroring her dive; she’s halfway down the passage before she catches herself and realizes she’s being used.
path_a.later_session_legacy: Session 3, Event 57 (Queen’s procession): the White Rabbit’s whisper is purely transactional—he hisses “Don’t let the Queen see you!” without eye contact, treating Alice like a hazard he’s already had to manage once.

path_b.immediate_effect: Alice slows long enough to fix the hedge’s crooked hawthorn, a flat stone like a stepping-slate, and the rabbit-hole’s position relative to the daisy patch; she drops her hair-ribbon at the lip as a marker, then slides in after him.
path_b.current_session_echo: In the fall she keeps testing orientation—counting seconds, watching which way the shelves drift—so in the Hall she first circles the room once, cataloguing: the tiny door, the key on the glass table, the curtain, the bottle; she speaks her fear out loud but keeps her hands steady.
path_b.next_session_payoff: Session 2, Event 24 (Rabbit mistakes her for Mary Ann): when the Rabbit points and orders, Alice pauses at the threshold of his house and deliberately repeats the instruction under her breath (“gloves… fan… Mary Ann”) like a checklist; that beat lets the player notice the name-label trap before committing to the errand.
path_b.later_session_legacy: Session 3, Event 57 (Queen’s procession): the Rabbit’s whisper becomes conspiratorial—he leans close and says, low, “If you keep your head down when she asks, you’ll pass,” acknowledging Alice as someone who pays attention and can follow a plan.

path_c.immediate_effect: Alice calls into the hedge—“Mr. Rabbit—wait!”—and for a single, uncanny beat the Rabbit half-turns; his red eyes flick to her, and he snaps, “No time!” before vanishing down the hole, forcing Alice to choose to follow someone who has explicitly refused her.
path_c.current_session_echo: The fall is colored by that rejection: she keeps replaying his glance and “No time,” so in the Hall she frames every object as a message from an unfriendly world—she reads the bottle label twice, checks the tiny door twice, and hesitates at the first irreversible action because she’s already been dismissed once.
path_c.next_session_payoff: Session 2, Event 24 (Rabbit mistakes her for Mary Ann): the moment the Rabbit addresses her, Alice fires back immediately—“I’m not Mary Ann!”—because she has prior proof he can hear her; the scene becomes a verbal tug-of-war in the doorway instead of silent compliance.
path_c.later_session_legacy: Session 3, Event 57 (Queen’s procession): the Rabbit’s whisper contains a grudging recognition—“You again”—before he offers help; it’s not warmth, but it is acknowledgement that she is a person who speaks back.

consequence_map_choice_2.tracked_dimension: self_definition_vs_external_labels (how you respond when authority’s role and costume are offered to you).

path_a.immediate_effect: Alice runs after the Rabbit into the corridor calling “sir—please!”; he doesn’t stop, but the word “sir” catches in the air, and she ends up alone holding his gloves and fan like items she’s been entrusted with.
path_a.current_session_echo: Back in the Hall sequence, she keeps defaulting to etiquette as a safety-rail—apologizing to the locked doors, thanking no one, and narrating herself as if being overheard by a stricter adult; when she later cries, it’s framed as ‘I ought not’ rather than ‘I won’t.’
path_a.next_session_payoff: Session 2, Event 24 (Rabbit mistakes her for Mary Ann): when he calls “Mary Ann,” Alice reflexively answers with politeness—“If you please, sir—”—and the Rabbit barrels on, using her courtesy as confirmation; the payoff is a specific humiliation beat where she hears herself accept the wrong role before she can stop it.
path_a.later_session_legacy: Session 4, Event 84 (called as witness): this path unlocks a ‘procedural’ posture—Alice begins testimony with careful forms (“If it please your Majesty…”) which buys her a few extra lines before the Court tries to silence her.

path_b.immediate_effect: Alice shouts after him—“Stop running from me!”—and her voice rings down the passage; the Rabbit flinches mid-stride (a visible hitch), then flees faster, leaving the gloves and fan behind as if he’s shedding responsibility.
path_b.current_session_echo: The rest of Session 1 is charged with defiant self-talk: in the Hall she speaks to herself like an ally, not a scold—‘I’m not going to cry again’—and when the world blocks her, she treats it as something to argue with (rattling the tiny door and saying, ‘That’s not fair’).
path_b.next_session_payoff: Session 2, Event 24 (Rabbit mistakes her for Mary Ann): Alice interrupts the order immediately—“Don’t call me that!”—and the Rabbit’s response escalates into visible panic about the Duchess (“She’ll have my head!”), making Alice’s self-definition directly responsible for raising the temperature of the scene.
path_b.later_session_legacy: Session 3, Event 55-58 (Queen’s questioning/croquet): this path supports bolder social stance—Alice is more likely to answer the Queen’s nonsense with plain contradiction, setting up a faster slide toward open conflict.

path_c.immediate_effect: Alice slips on the Rabbit’s gloves and keeps the fan moving; the cuffs sit too big, but the costume changes how she holds her shoulders—she looks, for a moment, like someone with an errand and a right to be here.
path_c.current_session_echo: In Session 1’s Hall, she starts treating props as passports: she keeps the gloves on while approaching the tiny door and later handles objects with a ‘this belongs to my role’ confidence; the identity spiral deepens because she’s literally wearing someone else’s hands.
path_c.next_session_payoff: Session 2, Event 24 (Rabbit mistakes her for Mary Ann): the misidentification lands cleanly—Alice doesn’t correct him at first, because the gloves already placed her in the servant-shape; the named payoff moment is her catching her reflection in the Rabbit’s hall mirror (or polished brass) and seeing ‘Mary Ann’ before she hears it spoken.
path_c.later_session_legacy: Session 3, Event 57 (Rabbit whisper in procession): the Rabbit assumes she’ll play along with roles; his whisper becomes instruction-heavy (“Stand there. Say nothing.”), and Alice is offered access in exchange for staying in costume.

session_close_design.resolution_prose:
You scoop up the dropped fan and gloves, and for a moment the hall feels less like a trap and more like a stage you can move across. The fan is cool against your fingers; it smells faintly of powder and something sharp—like crushed mint. You flutter it at your face and the heat eases, but your thoughts don’t. The garden’s bright promise still waits behind its tiny door, yet the word WAIT is suddenly louder than the lamps’ hum.

You try to anchor yourself the old way—facts, recitations, the tidy little hooks of schoolroom certainty—and they come loose in your mouth. Numbers refuse to line up. Capitals swap places as if they’ve been playing while you weren’t looking. Even your own voice sounds borrowed, as though it belongs to whichever girl you might be mistaken for.

Still, you are here. You are breathing. The fan keeps time. And the question that matters won’t be pushed back behind any curtain: if the world won’t agree on your name, what will you use to prove it to yourself?
session_close_design.hook_transition: For one quiet beat, you simply feel the air move—fan, breath, fan—like a small mercy you can control. Then, far down the passage, pattering footsteps return, quick and nervous, and you realize you may have to decide who you are before anyone else decides for you.

entry_point.detail: Cold open begins at the first true inciting tension: the Rabbit speaks, checks a watch, and Alice commits to pursuit toward the hedge/rabbit-hole. All low-stakes riverbank prologue is correctly cut.

emotional_promise.detail: First paragraph promises restless pursuit of the impossible (sensory heat baseline → uncanny Rabbit detail → immediate chase). The user is invited into motion, curiosity, and urgency.

first_choice_timing.detail: Branching Choice 1 is placed in the 0:00–2:30 setup window and is reachable within ~300 words of the cold open as designed.

consequential_choices.detail: Branching choices are mis-numbered/misaligned across the session: Beat Map labels Choice 2 as the DRINK ME posture, but Session Choice Design labels S1_C2 as the Rabbit/authority moment; Beat Map labels Choice 3 as the authority moment, but Session Choice Design labels S1_C3 as the identity-test end choice. This breaks verification against the consequence maps and cross-session payoff plan because it’s unclear which three choices are the canonical branching set for Session 1.

stakes_within_60_seconds.detail: Within the opening moments, the user understands: an impossible Rabbit is fleeing under time pressure (“I shall be late!”) and the opportunity will vanish if not pursued; the hedge shadow reads as a dangerous threshold.

moral_gray_area.detail: The authority/costume choice (plead politely vs confront vs opportunistically wear the gloves) is a genuine moral tradeoff with no correct answer and distinct downstream social/identity consequences.

entry_point_diagnosis.editorial_diagnosis:
The opening material before the cut is iconic but retention-risky for Session 2 because it replays long stretches of prior-session setup (bored riverbank, the fall, the hall puzzle, size changes, tears) without presenting *this session’s* immediate social-survival problem.

Paragraph-by-paragraph stakes test (as a new Session 2 user):
- Riverbank boredom + Rabbit sighting: curiosity, but no immediate consequence if you do/don’t act *in this session*.
- The fall: spectacle and whimsy; low agency and no pressing threat.
- Hall/doors/key/garden: clear goal, but it belongs to Session 1’s puzzle loop; for Session 2 it reads like recap.
- Drink/Eat size swings + crying pool: emotional texture, but still not a new, external pressure that demands a choice now.
- The first moment where stakes become legible inside 60 seconds for Session 2 is when the White Rabbit returns under deadline (“the Duchess… savage”), Alice is desperate enough to ask for help, and the Rabbit’s panic reaction creates a social power dynamic and a misrecognition setup.

So the session should begin at the Rabbit’s return: it instantly establishes (1) an authority figure with urgent obligations, (2) Alice’s vulnerability and need, and (3) a volatile social interaction where your approach (polite plea, bold stop, stealthy grab) can plausibly change how the ‘authority’ treats you—exactly the session’s core question about control, humiliation, and coercion.

format_specific_cut.cut_point: Source section: “Tears, Company, and a Misdelivered Errand — White Rabbit flees as Alice asks” (begins: “After a time she heard a little pattering of feet…”)
format_specific_cut.original_before_cut: Cuts all material prior to Event 24 in this session’s event list (Events 1–23). In the provided pages, that is everything up through Alice’s tears/pool and the lead-in before the Rabbit’s return.
format_specific_cut.cut_eliminates: Recap/setup: riverbank boredom, the fall, hall exploration, key/door/garden tease, Drink Me/Eat Me size mechanics, extended internal monologue, and the prolonged crying/pool sequence.
format_specific_cut.must_reintroduce: Through action (not exposition) you must quickly re-establish: (1) Alice is currently in an underground hall/passage; (2) size instability is a known danger; (3) she is emotionally raw/desperate; (4) the Rabbit is a status/authority figure with urgent social obligations (the Duchess).

entry_point_diagnosis.cold_open:
Hot air sticks to your cheeks as you kneel at the edge of your own tear-pool, fingers gritty with dust from the hall floor. You press your sleeve to your eyes anyway—because if you can stop the crying, you can start thinking.

Pattering footsteps skitter out of the dark. You freeze, listening. A shadow resolves into the White Rabbit—splendid coat, watch-chain flashing—clutching white kid gloves and a fan like they’re proof he belongs somewhere you don’t. “Oh! the Duchess, the Duchess!” he mutters, voice tight with panic. “She’ll be savage—”

You don’t wait for courage to arrive. You lift your chin and try to borrow his authority for a single breath. “If you please, sir—”

He startles as if you’ve struck him. The gloves and fan hit the floor. He bolts into the darkness, leaving you with what he dropped—and the question of what you do with it.
entry_point_diagnosis.emotional_promise: The emotional promise of this cold open is: humiliation. A user arrives feeling unsteady and wanting to regain control.

setup.source_moment: “After a time she heard a little pattering of feet…” → the White Rabbit returns panicking about the Duchess; Alice begs “If you please, sir—” and he startles, drops gloves/fan, and flees into darkness.
setup.why_it_qualifies: This is the first immediate, external pressure in Session 2: an authority figure under deadline collides with Alice’s desperation. It creates a legible social survival problem (how to approach power when you need something) and naturally delivers the first BRANCHING choice within the cold open: what does Alice do with the dropped status items / how does she pursue the Rabbit-shaped authority?

escalation.source_moment: Alice takes up the fan and gloves and begins fanning while walking/talking; identity anxiety spirals (“Who in the world am I?”) and she tests herself with recitations that come out wrong (math/geography/poem).
escalation.why_it_qualifies: Visible goal + obstacle become clear for this session’s arc: Goal = regain control (of self-presentation and composure) and leverage the Rabbit’s dropped ‘role props’ to navigate the next social encounter; Obstacle = destabilized identity/memory and the humiliating mismatch between what she knows she is and what Wonderland insists she is. Stakes compound because her internal incoherence will directly affect how she’s treated when the next authority figure labels her.

breath.source_moment: “Good-bye, feet!” / sending presents to one’s own feet with absurd address lines; self-mocking “Oh dear, what nonsense I’m talking!” (can be used as a short comedic ‘reset’ while she fans herself).
breath.why_it_qualifies: This is clean Carrollian absurdity that functions as a reward/release: the player gets a moment of playful wonder and humor (talking to her feet; imagining postage) after the humiliating Rabbit rejection and before heavier identity stakes harden. It also reinforces the theme (body/identity instability) without adding plot load.

twist.source_moment: Identity gate intensifies: “If I’m not the same, the next question is, Who in the world am I?” followed by the failed ‘proofs’ (tables/geography/recitation) and the looming possibility she’s “changed for Mabel.”
twist.why_it_qualifies: Moral-weight choice with no correct answer: does Alice cling to inherited ‘rules’ of correctness to prove she’s herself, treat wrongness as a system to experiment with, or surrender to observation/accept uncertainty? Each stance has costs: clinging deepens shame and brittleness; experimentation risks further alienation and danger; acceptance risks passivity and loss of agency. This is the session’s internal verdict pivot (“who am I willing to be under judgment?”) that will echo into later authority confrontations.

resolution.source_moment: The sequence ends mid-recitation (“I’ll try and say ‘How doth the little—’”), i.e., she is actively choosing a self-definition strategy in the moment after Rabbit’s misrecognition and before the next coercive social beat (being treated as someone else / ordered about).
resolution.why_it_qualifies: Primary arc resolved for this session slice: Alice moves from raw desperation to an actionable stance toward identity (a chosen method for ‘proving’/performing self). Seed for next session segment is planted: the stance determines how she handles being labeled/commanded (the ‘Mary Ann’ errand coercion beat) and how boldly she negotiates authority.

branching_choice_1.source_moment: White Rabbit drops the gloves and fan and flees into the dark hall as you call “If you please, sir—”.
branching_choice_1.what_this_choice_tracks: self_definition_vs_external_labels (how you respond when authority’s role and costume are offered to you).
branching_choice_1.narrative_setup: The Rabbit’s shoes patter away into the darkness like he’s being chased by the word Duchess itself. Your mouth is still open on sir, and the polite syllable hangs there, useless. On the floor, right where power slipped, lie his white kid gloves and his fan—clean, official, unmistakably his.
branching_choice_1.choice_question: What do you do with the Rabbit’s dropped status items?

option_a.text: You snatch up the gloves and fan and hurry after him, calling for him to wait.
option_a.downstream_effect: *You enter the next corridor already “in pursuit,” breath high and pride stung, and the story treats you as someone trying to regain face through action.*

option_b.text: You pick up the gloves and fan and put them on as neatly as you can before you follow.
option_b.downstream_effect: *You move forward wearing borrowed authority, and the story begins tracking how easily you let a costume speak for you.*

option_c.text: You leave the gloves and fan where they fell and watch the darkness a moment to see if he comes back.
option_c.downstream_effect: *You trade speed for information, and the story treats you as someone who refuses to be baited into a role you didn’t choose.*

branching_choice_1.all_paths_arrive_at: You end up in the hot hall again with the fan/gloves either in hand, on your body, or behind you, and the need to steady yourself presses in.

expressive_choices.source_moment: You fan yourself in the heat and try to get your breathing and thoughts under control as the identity spiral begins.
expressive_choices.narrative_lead_in: The hall’s air tastes like lamps and old dust, and the fan makes a small, desperate weather of your face. Your mind skitters between yesterday and today as if the difference is a crime you’re meant to explain. You can feel yourself choosing a tone for your own thoughts.
expressive_choices.choice_question: How do you speak to yourself as you walk?

option_a.text: You soothe yourself the way you would a younger child and keep your voice gentle inside your head.
option_a.downstream_effect: The narration leans tender and protective, turning your fear into something you can carry without flinching.

option_b.text: You scold yourself sharply and demand you pull yourself together at once.
option_b.downstream_effect: The narration turns brisk and biting, and every mistake lands like evidence against you.

option_c.text: You stop talking inwardly and focus on the hall itself—its doors, its shadows, its exits.
option_c.downstream_effect: The narration shifts outward and observational, making your body feel steadier even as your name feels slippery.

expressive_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: The question “Who in the world am I?” rises anyway, and you begin testing yourself against what you remember.

expressive_choices.source_moment: “Good-bye, feet!” absurdity beat; imagining mailing presents to your own feet with ridiculous address lines.
expressive_choices.narrative_lead_in: Your feet look impossibly far away, like they belong to someone taller and more important than you. The thought arrives fully formed and completely ridiculous: what if you simply posted them gifts and asked them, politely, to cooperate. It is nonsense—and it is, for one second, a relief.
expressive_choices.choice_question: What do you do with the nonsense when it shows up?

option_a.text: You commit to the bit and invent the full address, titles and all, as if the postal service is real here.
option_a.downstream_effect: The tone blooms into playful wonder, and the world feels briefly like it wants to be played with instead of survived.

option_b.text: You laugh at yourself out loud and let the laugh be the first honest breath you’ve had in minutes.
option_b.downstream_effect: The tone tilts wry and resilient, and humiliation loosens its grip for a beat.

option_c.text: You go quiet and stare at your feet as if they might stop being yours if you look away.
option_c.downstream_effect: The tone turns uncanny and bodily, making the distance between you and yourself feel charged rather than funny.

expressive_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: The relief passes, and you return to the harder question: if the world is wrong, how do you prove you’re still you?

expressive_choices.source_moment: You try to recite what you know—tables, geography, poems—and it comes out wrong as panic rises.
expressive_choices.narrative_lead_in: Numbers refuse to behave, capitals swap places like they’re playing a joke, and your mouth betrays you mid-line. The more you try to seize correctness, the more it slides through your fingers. You can feel a choice forming—not about facts, but about what you do when facts won’t hold still.
expressive_choices.choice_question: When you get an answer wrong, what do you do in the next heartbeat?

option_a.text: You repeat it immediately, faster, as if speed can bully it into being right.
option_a.downstream_effect: The tone becomes frantic and performative, like you’re trying to outrun embarrassment.

option_b.text: You slow down and pick apart what changed, treating the wrongness as a pattern to study.
option_b.downstream_effect: The tone becomes curious and analytical, turning failure into a kind of map.

option_c.text: You stop mid-sentence and listen to the silence, waiting for your body to tell you what it knows.
option_c.downstream_effect: The tone becomes inward and grounded, making breath and sensation feel like the only trustworthy evidence.

expressive_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: The fear of being ‘Mabel’ sharpens, and you must decide what counts as proof of self.

branching_choice_2.source_moment: Identity gate intensifies: you attempt ‘proof’ through school knowledge and it fails; the dread of being changed for Mabel rises.
values_in_tension: certainty (being correct) vs adaptability (learning the new rules)
dignity (not looking foolish) vs curiosity (following the weirdness)
agency (choosing who you are) vs surrender (accepting you may not know yet)
branching_choice_2.what_this_choice_tracks: rule_following_vs_rule_testing (how you decide to verify your identity when your memory and the world’s labels won’t agree).
branching_choice_2.narrative_setup: You can feel the old classroom inside you—the neat rows, the right answers, the safety of being able to prove yourself on demand. But Wonderland won’t hold still long enough to be graded. If you cannot even trust your own recitation, then who gets to decide what ‘Alice’ means: your lessons, the place you’re in, or the part of you that keeps watching?
branching_choice_2.choice_question: How do you try to verify who you are when your answers won’t come out right?

option_a.text: You cling to the old rules and force yourself to recite until something finally comes out correct.
option_a.downstream_effect: *You gain a brittle sense of control but take on sharper shame when you fail, and the story flags you as someone who seeks identity through correctness.*

option_b.text: You treat each wrong answer as a clue and start experimenting with new patterns instead of old lessons.
option_b.downstream_effect: *You trade comfort for momentum, and the story flags you as someone willing to rewrite the rules to stay yourself.*

option_c.text: You stop reciting and simply observe—breath, hands, voice—waiting to see what stays consistent.
option_c.downstream_effect: *You trade proof for presence, and the story flags you as someone who refuses to let panic dictate the terms of identity.*

branching_choice_2.moral_weight_confirmation: None of these choices guarantees safety or truth: correctness can become a cage, experimentation can deepen alienation, and observation can slide into passivity.
branching_choice_2.talkability_test: Players can argue whether ‘self’ is something you demonstrate (rules), discover (experiments), or inhabit (observation), especially under humiliating misrecognition.

branching_choice_3.source_moment: You hear the next authority call approaching (the errand setup) while holding/wearing the Rabbit’s gloves and fan; you must decide how to meet the coming misrecognition.
branching_choice_3.what_this_choice_tracks: compliance_vs_defiance (how you respond to arbitrary command when a role is imposed on you).
branching_choice_3.narrative_setup: Somewhere ahead, a voice cuts through the hall—sharp with expectation, as if you’re late and it’s your fault. The gloves and fan make you look like you belong to someone else’s errands, and part of you knows that being mistaken can be a shield or a trap. You have a single breath to choose your posture before the world names you.
branching_choice_3.choice_question: When the call comes for ‘Mary Ann,’ how do you step into the moment?

option_a.text: You answer at once and move toward the voice, deciding it’s safer to play the servant for now.
option_a.next_session_opens: Next session opens with you being hurried along as ‘Mary Ann,’ spoken to in orders, and you must survive the farce without losing your leverage.

option_b.text: You keep walking but do not answer, planning to redirect the mistake into a question you control.
option_b.next_session_opens: Next session opens with you half-caught between roles, trying to negotiate information while the authority grows impatient and closes distance.

option_c.text: You say clearly that you are not Mary Ann and refuse to be sent anywhere you didn’t choose.
option_c.next_session_opens: Next session opens with the mistake turning hostile, forcing you into immediate escalation as you resist being handled or herded.

session_close_design.resolution_prose:
You stop fanning long enough to feel your own wrist. The kid glove hangs loose there, absurdly proper, and the air it moves smells of lamp-oil and hot dust. You take a breath that is finally yours—no gulping, no sobbing, no panicked counting—and you look down.

Your feet are where they belong again. Not a mile away. Not vanished under a ceiling. Just feet: close, dependable, ready.

The hall is still the same long, low corridor of locked doors and hanging lamps, but you are no longer a mistake stretched or shrunken to fit it. You flex your fingers inside the white glove and feel the seams bite softly at your knuckles, proof that you can inhabit a shape without being swallowed by it.

For the first time since the fall, your name fits in your mouth without slipping. You are Alice—small enough to move, large enough to decide—and the question becomes quieter, sharper: if a place insists on calling you something else, do you let it?
session_close_design.hook_transition: For one heartbeat, you simply stand there and enjoy the ordinary weight of your body on the tiles. Then a voice slices down the passage ahead—brisk, impatient, as if you have already been late once and are about to be late again.

entry_point.detail: The cut begins at the first Session-2-specific pressure point: the Rabbit’s urgent return + misrecognition setup + dropped status items. This is the earliest moment with immediate social stakes and agency, and it avoids replaying Session 1’s puzzle loop.

first_choice_timing.detail: Branch 1 arrives by ~1:30–3:00 and is supported by a short cold open; it is clearly within 300 words of the start of the playable text as designed.

consequential_choices.detail: All three branching choices have planned downstream consequences with concrete next-session openings and later-session legacies: S2_C1 changes prop/costume framing and the Mary Ann misrecognition punchline; S2_C2 materially alters Caterpillar interrogation approach and later competence beats; S2_C3 cleanly gates three distinct next-session openings (compliance corridor advantage vs moving confrontation vs immediate escalation/chase) with cross-session echoes into Queen/Court authority handling.

stakes_within_60_seconds.detail: Within the opening beat, a panicked authority figure under deadline rejects you, drops high-status items, and flees—creating immediate stakes: you’re stranded, humiliated, and holding (or refusing) a social costume that can trap or shield you.

The consequence map specifies explicit scene beats and authored lines.

breath_beat.detail: A deliberate reward/release lands at 8:00–10:00 via the ‘Good-bye, feet!’ absurdity beat, with an expressive choice that lets the player savor wonder/wryness/uncanny bodily humor before the heavier identity gate.

exit_emotional_state.detail: The current resolution prose trends toward closure/arrival (‘For the first time since the fall, your name fits… You are Alice…’) which risks the user leaving with RESOLUTION rather than ANTICIPATION. The hook choice is strong, but the preceding paragraph resolves the internal question too cleanly.

talkability.detail: The session ends on a socially legible, arguable decision (‘Do you answer to Mary Ann?’) with three distinct self-concepts (survive by playing along, redirect to gain leverage, refuse and escalate). This is highly shareable and invites immediate comparison.

revision_instructions.instruction: Reduce the sense of internal completion in the final pre-choice paragraph. Replace/trim lines that declare identity ‘solved’ (e.g., ‘your name fits… You are Alice’) and instead leave identity as a sharpened, unresolved pressure that the Mary Ann call will test. Concrete edit: keep the embodied grounding (breath/feet) but pivot to doubt/edge—e.g., end on ‘Your name fits—until someone else says a different one’ and let the approaching voice feel like an imminent social verdict, not a new scene after closure.

entry_point_diagnosis.editorial_diagnosis:
For Session 3, the dramatic energy is not in the approach to the Duchess’s house, but in the moment Alice is suddenly forced to take responsibility for a vulnerable life in a hostile adult space.

Everything that would come before that (the footmen with the invitation; Alice deciding to enter; any exterior description) is *legible but low-stakes* for a new user in the first 60 seconds: it’s social color, not immediate jeopardy. A user can’t yet feel a clear personal risk, a clear goal, or a clear “what do I do right now?” problem.

The first paragraph where the answer becomes YES is the kitchen beat where violence and obligation collide: the room is choking, objects are flying, and the Duchess *physically hands you the baby* and leaves. That instantly creates (1) a concrete problem, (2) moral pressure (protect the child vs protect yourself), and (3) an action-forward situation that can deliver the first branching choice inside 300 words.

So: cut to the handoff. Start with the baby in your arms and the frying-pan in the air.

format_specific_cut.cut_point: Event 45 (Chapter 4, local pos 3): Smoky kitchen with Duchess and cook — the Duchess flings the baby to Alice and leaves; the cook throws a frying-pan.
format_specific_cut.cut_eliminates: Exterior approach/setup, servant/footman banter, and low-tension threshold-crossing into the house.
format_specific_cut.must_reintroduce: Through action and immediate context cues, re-establish: (1) you are Alice, newly competent at size-control but still outmatched socially; (2) this is the Duchess’s house and adult authority here is irrational/violent; (3) the Queen’s looming power (croquet invitation) as the reason this place matters.

entry_point_diagnosis.cold_open:
Smoke claws down your throat the instant you step inside; it reeks of pepper and scorched iron. Your shoes stick to a greasy floor, and somewhere close a pan shrieks against stone.

Before you can decide whether to retreat, a heavy woman in a cap whips around and *hurls a bundled baby into your arms*. The bundle is warm, squirming, and far too light—like it’s made of rags and breath. The Duchess’s grin is all teeth.

“Take it,” she snaps, already moving away, as if you’re furniture that’s learned to walk.

A cook, red-faced in the haze, answers by pitching a frying-pan after her. It spins through the smoke—straight across your line of sight—close enough that you feel the heat of it.

The Duchess is gone. The door is behind you. The baby writhes and wheezes against your chest.

If you don’t act now, something here is going to break.
entry_point_diagnosis.emotional_promise: The emotional promise of this cold open is: responsibility. A user arrives feeling unsettled and wanting to protect someone without losing their own safety.

setup.source_moment: Session 3 cut-point (Event 45 / Ch.4 kitchen): In the pepper-smoke chaos, the Duchess thrusts the baby into Alice’s arms as the cook hurls a frying-pan; the Duchess exits, leaving Alice holding a vulnerable life in a hostile room.
setup.why_it_qualifies: Immediate jeopardy + immediate obligation. Alice’s concrete problem is present in-frame (keep the baby safe / keep herself safe), and the scene naturally delivers a first BRANCHING choice inside 300 words (intervene vs retreat vs confront). It also re-establishes Wonderland’s adult authority as irrational and dangerous without preamble.

escalation.source_moment: Kitchen continues: the cook’s violence escalates; the Duchess’s casual cruelty normalizes harm; Alice must decide what to do with the baby and how to exit. Then transition to outside where the baby’s ‘wrongness’ becomes clearer (the bundle wheezes, twists, and begins to feel less like a human infant).
escalation.why_it_qualifies: Visible goal: get out of the Duchess’s house with the baby alive (or at least out of immediate danger) and avoid becoming a target. Clear obstacle: unpredictable adult violence (cook/objects), choking pepper-smoke, and social power imbalance—Alice is treated as furniture/servant. Stakes compound because every action risks provoking authority or harming the child.

breath.source_moment: Cheshire Cat encounter (Event 47 / Ch.6 direction-giving): the Cat appears/disappears in pieces, grinning, and offers absurdly calm ‘guidance’ about where to go and that everyone is mad.
breath.why_it_qualifies: Wonder/absurdity reward that relieves pressure after the kitchen/baby stress: a surreal, humorous conversational beat with playful logic and visual magic (vanishing body, lingering grin). It also functions as a player-facing reward: clarity that there are at least two viable paths forward (Hatter vs Hare / different kinds of madness).

twist.source_moment: Baby transforms into a pig as Alice carries it out (Ch.5 continuation from the Duchess episode): Alice realizes ‘saving’ it may mean releasing it; keeping it may be cruelty or danger.
twist.why_it_qualifies: Moral-weight, no correct answer: (a) hold on and try to protect/‘raise’ it despite its transformation (control, responsibility, denial), or (b) let it go into the wood (freedom, acceptance, but also abandonment). Either choice defines who Alice is becoming: caretaker vs survivor vs someone who refuses to force meaning onto Wonderland’s bodies.

resolution.source_moment: After the pig-baby decision, Alice is alone and must choose a direction based on the Cat’s counsel (toward the Tea Party path vs another route that still aims at the Garden/Queen’s sphere).
resolution.why_it_qualifies: Primary arc resolves: the immediate kitchen/baby crisis is concluded (baby outcome decided; Alice exits the hostile adult space). Seed for what follows is planted as an unresolved player decision about which social ‘madness’ to enter next—setting up the next session’s social-rule navigation and complicity pressure (Tea Party → Garden → Queen’s court).

branching_choice_1.narrative_setup: The kitchen is a storm of pepper-smoke and flying iron, loud enough to swallow thought. The Duchess jams a wheezing bundle into your arms as if it is nothing more than a parcel, then sweeps out and leaves you standing in the blast. The cook’s eyes flick to you, to the baby, to the next thing she can throw. The child squirms against your ribs, hot and wrong and helpless.

option_a.text: You step between the cook and the baby and command her to stop.
option_a.downstream_effect: *The cook’s attention locks on you, and the room’s violence turns personal as you become the obstacle.*

option_b.text: You shield the baby with your body and edge toward the nearest exit without a word.
option_b.downstream_effect: *You take the safest line out, but the baby’s distressed noises risk drawing pursuit and accusations as you slip away.*

option_c.text: You hold still and watch the cook’s rhythm, waiting for the cleanest moment to move.
option_c.downstream_effect: *You buy a sliver of control, learning where danger actually comes from, at the cost of letting the chaos continue around the child.*

branching_choice_1.all_paths_arrive_at: The kitchen remains unstable, and you must get out with the bundle while objects keep flying and the pepper-smoke bites your throat.

expressive_choices.source_moment: Kitchen escalation: pepper-smoke, thrown objects, baby wheezing; Alice tries to function under sensory overload.
expressive_choices.narrative_lead_in: Pepper stings your eyes until the world swims, and the baby’s breath rasps like it’s trying to remember how to be air. Every clang of metal makes you flinch, and you feel your own lungs start to argue with the room.
expressive_choices.choice_question: How do you steady yourself enough to act?

option_a.text: You speak to the baby in a low, steady stream of nonsense just to keep your hands from shaking.
option_a.downstream_effect: Your narration turns tender and rhythmic, making the baby feel like a responsibility you chose rather than a trap you were handed.

option_b.text: You count exits, weapons, and distances like a lesson, forcing the room into numbers.
option_b.downstream_effect: Your narration turns crisp and practical, framing Wonderland’s cruelty as a problem to solve rather than a mood to endure.

option_c.text: You go quiet and let the room show you its rules before you give it any of your fear.
option_c.downstream_effect: Your narration turns watchful and restrained, emphasizing control through observation while the tension hums under your silence.

expressive_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: A gap opens in the violence, and you move—one way or another—toward getting out of the Duchess’s house.

expressive_choices.source_moment: Outside transition: Alice has escaped the house; the bundle’s 'wrongness' becomes undeniable as it shifts and wheezes.
expressive_choices.narrative_lead_in: Outside, the air is clearer, but the bundle in your arms is not. It is heavier than it should be, jointed in odd places, and it makes a sound that is less a cry than a stubborn grunt trying to become itself.
expressive_choices.choice_question: What do you tell yourself about what you’re carrying?

option_a.text: You insist it is a baby and you will treat it like one until it proves otherwise.
option_a.downstream_effect: Your narration leans protective and humanizing, pushing back against Wonderland by naming the thing as a child.

option_b.text: You admit you don’t know what it is and focus only on keeping it alive for the next minute.
option_b.downstream_effect: Your narration becomes survivalist and present-tense, narrowing the world to breath, grip, and forward motion.

option_c.text: You assume Wonderland is changing it on purpose and you start looking for who benefits.
option_c.downstream_effect: Your narration turns suspicious and analytical, treating the transformation as a social move rather than a natural one.

expressive_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: The bundle shifts again, more violently this time, forcing you to confront that the change is accelerating.

expressive_choices.source_moment: Cheshire Cat encounter (Event 47 / Ch.6 direction-giving): the Cat appears/disappears in pieces, grinning, and offers absurdly calm guidance about where to go and that everyone is mad.
expressive_choices.narrative_lead_in: A grin appears in the boughs as if the tree has decided to mock you, and then the rest of a cat arrives in installments. It speaks as though giving directions is the simplest kindness in the world, and vanishes mid-sentence just to prove it can. The grin lingers, pleased with itself.
expressive_choices.choice_question: How do you answer a creature that treats reality like a joke?

option_a.text: You ask politely for the quickest safe route, as if the Cat is a sensible guide.
option_a.downstream_effect: The tone stays mannerly and strangely domestic, making the Cat’s madness feel like bad etiquette rather than danger.

option_b.text: You challenge the Cat to explain what it means by “mad,” refusing to let it dodge with riddles.
option_b.downstream_effect: The tone becomes sparring and bright, turning the conversation into a contest of definitions you intend to win.

option_c.text: You let the Cat talk and simply watch what appears and disappears, waiting for the useful part.
option_c.downstream_effect: The tone becomes wry and observational, letting the Cat’s magic land as wonder while you keep your guard up.

expressive_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: The Cat’s guidance leaves you with two clear directions forward—two different kinds of social madness—and the bundle in your arms keeps shifting.

branching_choice_2.source_moment: Baby transforms into a pig as Alice carries it out (Ch.5 continuation from the Duchess episode): Alice realizes ‘saving’ it may mean releasing it; keeping it may be cruelty or danger.
branching_choice_2.what_this_choice_tracks: empathy_vs_self_preservation (how you define care when the vulnerable becomes inconvenient, dangerous, or non-human).
branching_choice_2.narrative_setup: The bundle writhes hard enough to bruise your forearms, and its face—if it is a face—pushes out of the cloth with a snout where a mouth should be. It makes a pleased, stubborn sound that is not a human cry, and its little legs kick with new certainty. You can keep holding on and force your idea of “safe” onto it, or you can stop pretending you know what it needs. The wood ahead is open, indifferent, and full of places to vanish.
branching_choice_2.choice_question: What do you do with the pig-baby?

option_a.text: You keep it in your arms and commit to protecting it, whatever it becomes.
option_a.downstream_effect: *You remain visibly burdened and conspicuous, and the next social encounters read you as someone carrying a problem they did not ask for.*

option_b.text: You set it down gently and let it go, trusting it to belong to the wood more than to you.
option_b.downstream_effect: *You walk on alone with relief and guilt in equal measure, and your next encounters have fewer immediate liabilities but sharper loneliness.*

option_c.text: You wait and watch a moment longer, releasing only when it chooses a direction on its own.
option_c.downstream_effect: *You try to make the decision about its agency instead of your comfort, but you risk losing time and control as it bolts unpredictably.*

branching_choice_2.moral_weight_confirmation: Each option can be defended as care: holding on risks cruelty through control, letting go risks harm through abandonment, and waiting risks danger through hesitation.
branching_choice_2.talkability_test: Players can argue for days about whether ‘care’ means custody, freedom, or respect for agency when the creature stops fitting the category of ‘baby.’

branching_choice_3.source_moment: After the pig-baby decision, Alice is alone and must choose a direction based on the Cat’s counsel (toward the Tea Party path vs another route that still aims at the Garden/Queen’s sphere).
branching_choice_3.what_this_choice_tracks: impulse_vs_deliberation (how you commit to a path through social madness when given imperfect information).
branching_choice_3.narrative_setup: The Cat’s grin is gone now, but its logic hangs in the air: if you go one way, you meet one kind of mad; if you go the other, you meet another. The trees don’t mark roads so much as they offer openings, and you can feel the world waiting to define you by whichever door you choose to walk through. Somewhere ahead is the Garden and the Queen’s orbit, but you have to pick what you’ll risk first to get there. Your feet start forward, then stop, as if they want your permission.
branching_choice_3.choice_question: Which direction do you commit to next?

option_a.text: You head straight for the Tea Party, choosing the loudest madness to learn its rules fast.
option_a.next_session_opens: Next session opens with you arriving at a table that treats manners like weapons, and you must earn a seat without becoming the joke.

option_b.text: You take the quieter route toward the Garden, choosing distance and caution over confrontation.
option_b.next_session_opens: Next session opens with you navigating a deceptively calm approach where small signs hide big threats, and you must decide when to reveal yourself.

option_c.text: You stay where you are and watch the paths for a signal, refusing to be rushed into someone else’s story.
option_c.next_session_opens: Next session opens with the world coming to you—an encounter finds you first—and you must respond without the comfort of having chosen the terrain.

consequence_map_choice_1.tracked_dimension: empathy_vs_self_preservation (how you act when a vulnerable life is placed in your arms inside an abusive adult space).

path_a.immediate_effect: The cook snaps her head toward you and hurls the frying-pan at your chest instead of across the room; you twist sideways and the pan rings off the doorframe, blocking the nearest exit with hot iron.
path_a.current_session_echo: You leave the kitchen with the cook shouting after you—“Thief! Baby-thief!”—so every later social beat carries a faint accusation: at the Cat’s tree you keep glancing back; at the Tea Party the March Hare opens with, “Been stealing infants, have you?” before offering tea.
path_a.next_session_payoff: Session 4, Event 84–85 (called as witness): the King squints at you over his spectacles and reads from a scrap: “Witness previously interfered in domestic discipline (see Cook’s statement).” You get a specific higher-risk testimony line: “I stepped between her and the child because she was throwing pans,” which immediately provokes the Queen’s “Off with her head!” escalation beat.
path_a.later_session_legacy: Session 4, Event 88 (trial erupts): when Alice challenges Rule Forty-two, the Cook—present among the crowd—adds a shouted heckle, “She’s always interfering!” making your courtroom defiance feel like a continuation of that first intervention.

path_b.immediate_effect: You back toward the doorway with the bundle tucked under your chin; a pepper-box skitters underfoot, you slip, and a falling saucepan clips your shoulder—painful but you keep your grip and get out into the clearer air.
path_b.current_session_echo: Because you didn’t confront the cook, you exit with less noise but more physical strain: your shoulder throbs through the Cat scene, and at the Tea Party you accept the Dormouse’s “No room!” with a tighter, more guarded posture—your dialogue options skew toward short, self-protective replies rather than moral commentary.
path_b.next_session_payoff: Session 4, Event 84–85: when you’re called as witness, there is no ‘Cook’s statement’ prop. Instead, the King opens with, “You were present, yet you avoided involvement—why?” unlocking a safer, procedural answer: “I removed myself from danger,” which reduces immediate Queen-agro but costs you credibility with any threatened minor characters in the gallery.

path_c.immediate_effect: You freeze long enough to clock the cook’s throwing pattern—pan, pepper, then ladle—then you move on the ladle-beat; you slip past as the ladle arcs wide and clatters into the hearth, buying a clean lane to the door.
path_c.current_session_echo: Your ‘pattern-reading’ becomes your social mode: at the Cat you ask one extra clarifying question (“Which way is safest?”) before committing; at the Tea Party you watch the Hatter’s timing and choose when to speak, producing a noticeably more strategic, less reactive version of the same scenes.
path_c.next_session_payoff: Session 4, Event 85–86: during the Rule Forty-two dispute, you get a uniquely tactical objection: you point out the Court’s sequence error—“Sentence first—verdict afterwards”—explicitly mirroring how you survived the kitchen by reading rhythm, not by fighting or fleeing.
path_c.later_session_legacy: Session 4, Event 89 (awakening): Alice’s sister recalls Alice’s ‘odd, watchful pauses’ in the dream-coda narration variant, framing your Wonderland self as observant rather than merely brave or merely frightened.

consequence_map_choice_2.tracked_dimension: empathy_vs_self_preservation (how you define care when the vulnerable becomes inconvenient, dangerous, or non-human).

path_a.immediate_effect: You keep the pig-baby clutched to your apron; it squeals and wriggles free enough to smear your hands with mud, and you have to stop and re-wrap it, making you late to the next landmark the Cat indicated.
path_a.current_session_echo: You arrive to the next social encounter visibly burdened: at the Tea Party table, the March Hare points at your bundle—“You can’t bring that here”—and the Hatter insists on inspecting it ‘for manners,’ turning the tea ‘reward’ beat into a tense comedy of protecting what you chose to carry.
path_a.next_session_payoff: Session 4, Event 63–66 (Mock Turtle interlude): the Gryphon interrupts the Mock Turtle’s sobbing to say, “She’s the one who carried a pig like it was a baby.” The Mock Turtle’s line—“That’s the sort of thing that makes you melancholy”—becomes a direct validation of your caretaking choice and opens a sympathy-forward exchange before the trial pressure returns.
path_a.later_session_legacy: Session 4, Event 84 (witness called): the Queen uses it as character evidence—“This one collects creatures”—which can either be weaponized against you (if you resist) or used as leverage (“Then you’ll speak, for the good of the Court”) depending on your compliance/defiance state.

path_b.immediate_effect: You set the pig down at the wood’s edge; it snorts once, then trots off into the bracken, and the sudden absence leaves your arms aching with phantom weight.
path_b.current_session_echo: You hit the next beats unencumbered but lonelier: the Cat’s grin feels colder, and at the Tea Party the ‘reward’ lands cleaner—more room to play with the absurdity—yet your internal monologue keeps flashing to the pig’s retreating back, tinting humor with guilt.
path_b.next_session_payoff: Session 4, Event 63–64: the Mock Turtle’s ‘beautiful soup’ lament includes a tailored barb from the Gryphon—“She lets things go when they squeal”—which stings; your payoff is a self-justifying line you can choose: “I set it down gently. I didn’t throw it away,” explicitly naming the care you believe you gave.
path_b.later_session_legacy: Session 4, Event 89–90 (waking frame): in the sister-coda variant, Alice’s remembered tone is sharper and more defensive when recounting ‘the pig-baby,’ reflecting that you chose mercy-through-release rather than custody.

path_c.immediate_effect: You wait; the pig-baby’s ears twitch, then it bolts suddenly between your feet and nearly trips you, forcing you to lunge and grab a handful of cloth—either you steady yourself or you fall to one knee in the leaves, losing precious seconds.
path_c.current_session_echo: Time and control feel compromised: you reach the next scene slightly flustered, so at the Tea Party you’re more easily wrong-footed by the Hatter’s riddles; the comedy beat turns into a ‘caught off-balance’ version of the same content, emphasizing your attempt to honor the creature’s agency but paying in social footing.
path_c.next_session_payoff: Session 4, Event 84–85: when pressed as witness, you get a distinctive ‘agency’ argument: “I waited to see what it chose,” which the King treats as irrelevant (“Creatures don’t choose in evidence”), letting you explicitly clash with the Court’s dehumanizing logic—an on-theme payoff for choosing to respect the pig’s direction.

consequence_map_choice_3.tracked_dimension: impulse_vs_deliberation (how you commit to a path through social madness when given imperfect information).

path_a.immediate_effect: N/A — session ends on this choice.
path_a.next_session_opening: Session 4 opens on the long tea-table: the March Hare and Hatter slide sideways to block empty places while chanting “No room!” and you must choose whether to force a seat, negotiate for one, or walk the table’s length under their commentary.
path_a.next_session_payoff: Within the first 5 minutes of Session 4, the Hatter demands, “Say what you mean,” and you get an authored validation beat: because you chose the loudest madness to learn rules fast, you can answer with a crisp, rule-testing line that wins you a begrudging ‘seat’ (a cup shoved toward you) rather than becoming the joke.
path_a.later_session_legacy: At the later Court sequence, the Hatter recognizes you as ‘the one from tea’ and interrupts testimony with a nonsequitur that either helps (distracts the Queen) or harms (derails your objection), keeping your chosen route as a persistent social connection.

path_b.immediate_effect: N/A — session ends on this choice.
path_b.next_session_opening: Session 4 opens on a deceptively quiet approach to the Garden: painted rose-trees ahead, three card-gardeners on tiptoe, and the first threat is soft—whispered—before it becomes official.
path_b.next_session_payoff: Early Session 4, you catch the Gardeners mid-panic and can leverage your cautious approach: you get a ‘reveal timing’ payoff where staying hidden for one extra exchange lets you overhear “It was meant to be red,” giving you a precise, practical way to help (offering paint) rather than stumbling into the Queen’s wrath blind.
path_b.later_session_legacy: In the Trial, one of those same card-gardeners appears in the gallery and, if you helped discreetly, slides you a tiny paintbrush as a silent ‘thank you’ prop—an object callback that can be used as a visual beat during your witness moment.

path_c.immediate_effect: N/A — session ends on this choice.
path_c.next_session_opening: Session 4 opens with the terrain choosing you: the Queen’s croquet procession bursts through the trees first, and a card-soldier physically redirects you into line—no time to scout, no time to choose allies.
path_c.next_session_payoff: In the opening confrontation, you get a payoff that validates refusing to be rushed: you can truthfully say, “I didn’t come to you—you came to me,” which momentarily stalls the Queen’s narrative control and forces a procedural beat (the King asking, “Who is this?”) before the menace resumes.
path_c.later_session_legacy: Because you didn’t choose a ‘faction’ scene (tea or gardeners) first, later allies are thinner: at the Trial, no Hatter familiarity and no gardener gratitude—your witness sequence plays more isolated, emphasizing self-reliance as the lasting signature of this path.

session_close_design.resolution_prose:
You stand with your palms still smelling faintly of pepper and warm cloth, the last of the Duchess’s kitchen noise finally behind you. The air here is different—cooler, leaf-filtered—like someone has put a damp hand over a candle. Ahead, between two roots, you glimpse it again: not the whole Garden, but a slice of it, impossibly bright. A fountain’s thin hiss reaches you, and with it the clean, green bite of wet stone.

For a moment you simply breathe. You are not holding the baby anymore; you are not being ordered to admire cruelty as if it were good manners. Your feet are your own. The path does not shove you.

Somewhere above, a branch creaks as if it is smiling. The Cat’s counsel—cool, useless, and somehow true—hangs in your ear: one way, one kind of mad; the other way, another. The Garden is still ahead, but what you risk first is up to you.
session_close_design.hook_transition: You let the quiet settle in your ribs, the fountain-sound steady as a promise. Then the leaves shift, and the silence becomes a stage: three openings between trunks, each like a doorway waiting to decide what you are.
